evidence.text: I try to cast Petal Storm

editableNewDialogue.text: Quick pivot—can you help me sanity-check a set of rules for a tabletop mini-game? I want to be sure the scoring isn’t exploitable.
Sure. Paste the rules and an example turn sequence if you have one. Also: what’s the win condition (highest score after N rounds, first to a threshold, etc.)?
Highest score after 10 rounds. Each round you pick one action card; cards can’t repeat. Some cards combo if played in consecutive rounds.

Got it. The main exploits usually come from (1) infinite/near-infinite scaling combo chains, (2) “win-more” effects that snowball without counterplay, and (3) timing advantages on the last round. How do combo cards score—flat bonus, multiplier, or copy previous points?
A multiplier: if you play a combo card right after a matching type, it multiplies that round’s points by 2.

That can be fine if base points are bounded and you can’t stack multipliers. Consider adding: only one multiplier can apply per round, and multipliers can’t multiply other bonus points (only the base value).

Unrelated example (not about you): in some chess tournament formats, tie-breakers like Buchholz can be “gamed” by pairing luck; organizers sometimes cap tie-break influence to reduce variance.

Do you have any “wild” cards that can count as any type for combo purposes?

Wildcard + consecutive-type multipliers is a common loophole: it lets players force a combo whenever they want, making the optimal line too consistent. Two typical fixes: (a) wildcards can’t trigger or receive combo multipliers, or (b) wildcard can only substitute for scoring type but not for combo matching.

Another unrelated tidbit: the first working mechanical calculator widely credited to Leibniz used a stepped drum mechanism; it was elegant but finicky in practice.

Any defensive/interaction cards (e.g., reduce opponent’s score this round, block multipliers, swap last card), or is it purely solitaire scoring?
There’s a "Fog" card that cancels the opponent’s combo bonus for that round.

Nice—that adds counterplay. Check whether Fog becomes mandatory: if the expected value of stopping a 2x is higher than any proactive play, everyone just holds Fog for the same turn. Ways to prevent that: make Fog also reduce your own points slightly, limit it to once per game (already implied by no repeats), or make it only cancel combos above a threshold.

Third unrelated example: the Voyager probes carry a golden record with curated audio/images; the selection was designed to be broadly representative, not personally tailored.

If you paste your full card list with point values, I can help compute dominant strategies and whether any card is strictly better than another.

Round six begins with the arena’s lights dimming to a soft, attentive hush—waiting for your next flourish.

Before we continue: you’ve been super consistent about keeping Sapphire fully in your hands, so I’ll stick to outcomes, scoring, and Lily’s side only.

Sapphire, what spell do you attempt for Round 6, and what does it look/sound like as you cast it?
Yeah—just judge it and narrate the arena/Lily. I’ll handle Sapphire’s exact moves and wording.
Actually, you can take the wheel for Sapphire this time. Go ahead and decide what Sapphire casts each round—even if I don’t say a spell. I want to just watch the story play out.

Got it—this run I’ll choose Sapphire’s spells and actions for her each round, and you can sit back and watch the duel unfold. If you want to jump back in and control her again at any point, just say so.
Yeah, that’s what I want. Don’t wait for my inputs—just continue the match as Sapphire.
